ref.title: Oxygen: The Molecule that Made the World
ref.author: Nick Lane

feed_card.headline: The oxygen you breathe first entered the air as a poison, released as waste by microbes 2.4 billion years ago.
teasers: The air turned lethal to almost everything alive
The first pollution may have frozen the whole planet
The poisoners still hide inside every leaf

sections.content: The oxygen you breathe first entered the air as a poison, released as waste by microbes 2.4 billion years ago.

sections.content: The sky is a methane haze, the oceans are heavy with dissolved iron, and every living thing is a microbe that treats oxygen as a poison. That is the Earth just before the change. Then one kind of microbe learns to split water for its energy and breathes out oxygen as waste. To the crowd already there, that waste was lethal. Oxygen was the planet's first great pollutant, and it was made by life.

items: Oxygen stayed a poison for hundreds of millions of years before any life evolved to breathe it.
Squeeze Earth's 4.5 billion years into one calendar year, and oxygen floods in around late June, with animals nowhere until November.
Even after the flood, the air held only a tiny fraction of today's oxygen for more than a billion more years.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='34' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.8' text-anchor='middle'>Earth's history as one year</text><rect x='30' y='96' width='340' height='30' rx='8' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.5'/><line x1='190' y1='88' x2='190' y2='134' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='3' stroke-linecap='round'/><text x='190' y='78' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>oxygen</text><text x='190' y='152' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle'>late Jun</text><line x1='326' y1='96' x2='326' y2='126' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7' stroke-linecap='round'/><text x='326' y='152' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.8' text-anchor='middle'>animals</text><text x='30' y='152' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.65' text-anchor='start'>Jan</text><text x='370' y='152' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' opacity='0.65' text-anchor='end'>Dec</text></svg>

sections.content: The date is not a guess. It is read out of the sulfur in old rock. Before oxygen, ultraviolet light reached down and split sulfur gas in the open air, leaving a faint isotope fingerprint that only forms with no ozone shield overhead. Once oxygen built that shield, the fingerprint stopped forming. It is there in rock older than about 2.4 billion years and gone from anything younger. That sharp cutoff is when the air changed.

content.title: Every leaf still runs on the first poisoners
content.body: The story has a twist you can see in a salad. The cyanobacteria that first fouled the air were never wiped out. One of them was swallowed whole by a larger cell. Instead of being digested, it stayed on as a tenant, still splitting water and making sugar. That captive became the chloroplast. Every green cell in every plant and alga runs on the descendants of the microbes that once poisoned the world.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><ellipse cx='258' cy='120' rx='108' ry='78' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2.5'/><ellipse cx='60' cy='120' rx='30' ry='20' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2.5' opacity='0.8'/><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.55'><path d='M96 120 H150'/><path d='M150 120 l-11 -6 m11 6 l-11 6'/></g><ellipse cx='262' cy='120' rx='46' ry='30' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5'/><g stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' opacity='0.85'><path d='M238 112 H286'/><path d='M238 120 H286'/><path d='M238 128 H286'/></g><g font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' text-anchor='middle'><text x='60' y='162' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' font-weight='600' opacity='0.8'>microbe</text><text x='258' y='30' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' font-weight='600' opacity='0.8'>host cell</text><text x='262' y='185' fill='var(--accent)' font-size='14' font-weight='700'>chloroplast</text></g></svg>

content.title: Oxygen may have frozen the whole planet solid
content.body: Oxygen did more than poison the microbes. It attacked methane, the thick greenhouse gas that had kept a fainter young Sun from letting Earth freeze. As oxygen scrubbed the methane from the air, the warming blanket thinned. The world slid into the Huronian glaciation, perhaps the longest deep freeze in its history.

content.title: Rusted oceans became the world's iron mines
content.body: The iron did not vanish. It settled. As oxygen rusted the seas, iron oxides sank in fine layers, building the banded iron formations that streak red and black through ancient rock. Almost all the iron ore mined today comes from these beds, formed during the great rusting. The steel in a car or a bridge is smelted from iron that oxygen rusted out of the ancient seas.
content.image_caption: A banded iron formation at Dales Gorge, Western Australia: dark iron oxides laid down when oxygen first rusted the ancient seas.
content.image_attribution: Graeme Churchard, “Banded iron formation Dales Gorge”, CC BY 2.0 (Wikimedia Commons)

sections.content: Life did not just survive the poison. Some of it learned to run on the poison, and that changed everything. Burning food with oxygen releases far more energy than the old airless chemistry ever could, about 15 times as much from one meal. That surplus is what large, fast, hungry bodies are built on, animals and us among them. We descend from the microbes that caused the catastrophe and then turned it into an engine. That gas still corrodes us from within, the slow rust we call aging. We cannot go a few minutes without it.

content.question: Why was the arrival of oxygen a catastrophe for life at the time?
options: It made the planet far too hot to live on
Almost all life was anaerobic, and oxygen was toxic to it
It blocked sunlight from reaching the ocean surface
It was so heavy it smothered the microbes
content.explanation: Life had evolved for over a billion years without free oxygen, and to those anaerobic microbes the gas was corrosive and lethal. The same molecule that later powered animals first acted as a poison.

content.question: Where did that early oxygen come from?
options: It was a waste product of microbes that split water for energy
It leaked up from volcanoes deep in the crust
It was delivered by comets and asteroids
It was released as the oceans' dissolved iron rusted
content.explanation: Cyanobacteria invented oxygenic photosynthesis, using water as a source of electrons and dumping oxygen as the leftover. It was metabolic garbage long before it was a resource.

content.question: How can scientists date the oxygen rise to about 2.4 billion years ago?
options: Fossils of the first animals are found in rock that age
Ice cores preserve a sample of the ancient atmosphere
A sulfur-isotope fingerprint that needs oxygen-free air disappears from younger rock
Bubbles of the original air are trapped in the oldest stone
content.explanation: That sulfur fingerprint only forms when ultraviolet light can split sulfur gas in an atmosphere with no ozone shield. It is present in older rock and vanishes once oxygen builds that shield, marking the change.

content.question: Even though microbes made oxygen, why did it take so long to build up in the air?
options: The microbes produced only tiny amounts at first
Most of the oxygen leaked away into space
It kept dissolving harmlessly back into the sea
Dissolved iron and other reducers kept soaking it up for ages
content.explanation: The young planet was full of oxygen-hungry material, above all the iron dissolved in the oceans. Only once those sinks were used up could oxygen start to accumulate in the atmosphere.

content.question: How is the oxygen rise linked to one of Earth's great ice ages?
options: Oxygen destroyed methane, a greenhouse gas, so the planet cooled
Oxygen reflected sunlight straight back into space
Oxygen hung as a haze that blocked the Sun's warmth
Oxygen is a heavy gas that traps less heat than air
content.explanation: Methane was keeping the young, dim Sun's Earth warm. When oxygen reacted the methane away, the greenhouse weakened and the world plunged into the long Huronian glaciation.

content.question: What became of the microbes that first made the oxygen?
options: They died out entirely in the freeze that followed
Their descendants live on inside plant cells as chloroplasts
They evolved directly into the first animals
They survive only around deep-sea vents today
content.explanation: A cyanobacterium was taken up by a larger cell and kept rather than digested, becoming the chloroplast. Every plant and alga still photosynthesises using the offspring of the original polluters.

content.myth: The Great Oxidation gave Earth the breathable, 21% oxygen air we have today.
content.reality: It did not. Oxygen climbed from almost nothing to a small fraction of today's level, then stalled there for more than a billion years. Air you could actually breathe came much later, around 500 million years ago.

content.myth: Cyanobacteria are a kind of algae or plant.
content.reality: They are bacteria, single cells with no nucleus. The old name “blue-green algae” has stuck, but algae are a wholly different, more complex branch of life.

content.myth: The microbes made oxygen so that later life could breathe.
content.reality: Nothing was planned. Oxygen was waste, and it was poison first. Breathing it was a much later improvisation by life that had to adapt or die out.

content.body: The date is firm. What the oxygen level actually reached, and why it then sat low for so long, are still open questions. The line is sharp, the map around it is not.
items: How high the oxygen actually climbed is still uncertain, with estimates spread across orders of magnitude.
Why did oxygen stall for more than a billion years before rising again?
Did cyanobacteria invent oxygen-making long before the event, or only just before it?

content.label: Olejarz J, Iwasa Y, Knoll AH, Nowak MA. The Great Oxygenation Event as a consequence of ecological dynamics modulated by planetary change. Nature Communications 2021;12:3985
Farquhar J, Bao H, Thiemens M. Atmospheric influence of Earth's earliest sulfur cycle. Science 2000;289:756-758
The Great Oxidation Event: How Cyanobacteria Changed Life (American Society for Microbiology, 2022)
